Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd just kind of wing it whenever I remembered or felt like it. Sometimes I'd sit down with the intention to study but end up scrolling through my phone instead. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so sitting and reading stuff online never clicked for me. Most weeks I'd maybe put in some hours here and there, but nothing consistent.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, mostly driven by deadlines when they were breathing down my neck. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble. Regular times? That wasn't really my thing. Life stuff kept getting in the way, and honestly, I just couldn't find the motivation to stick to a schedule.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting an email reminder that something was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I'd missed anything major. It wasn't like I was excited to check in or anything. I'd often think about logging in but then just... not. The prompts from the system were pretty much the only thing that got me there.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became automatic for me like checking social media or whatever. I tried a few times to make it a habit, but it just didn't stick. The platform felt kind of sterile and I'd lose interest pretty quick. I guess I logged in enough times that I knew my way around, but it never felt natural.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I could at least watch something instead of just reading walls of text. I learn better when I can see things being done or explained visually. The discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that made more sense than the official materials. But honestly, nothing really grabbed me.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and any extra resources they posted. They felt like busywork and I didn't have the energy to dig through all of it. The optional stuff was the first to go when I was feeling overwhelmed. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. With limited resources at home, I just couldn't justify spending hours on things that weren't graded.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, then maybe I'd do it. Anything that felt abstract or like it was just there to fill time, I'd skip. I needed to feel like my effort was going to pay off somehow. With everything else going on, I had to pick my battles, you know?
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a suggestion I could ignore, so I usually did. Required activities had consequences so they got priority, even if I did them last minute. Optional things felt like extras for students who had their lives together, which wasn't me. I figured if it wasn't mandatory, it probably wasn't that important anyway.
Q9: Sometimes I'd download stuff and look at it on my phone when I had time, like on the bus or whatever. I also tried watching some YouTube videos on topics when the course materials weren't making sense. None of that would show up in the system obviously. I'm a hands-on person so I'd try to find practical examples outside the course too.
Q10: There were times I'd be thinking through problems in my head or talking about course stuff with a friend, but I wasn't logged in anywhere. I'd also review downloaded notes without being online. The LMS wouldn't know I spent an hour trying to understand a concept by myself. A lot of my processing happens away from the screen.
Q11: Yeah, definitely. I'd open something once, download it or screenshot it, and then work from that. No point staying logged in and eating up data when I could just save it. The system would show I looked at it for like two minutes but I might've spent way longer with it offline. That happened pretty regularly actually.
Q12: Around midterms everything just piled up and I kind of checked out. I was dealing with some personal stuff and money was tight, which made it hard to focus on school. There were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to care about the course. The CBT sessions helped me recognize some patterns but by then I'd already fallen behind.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed about other things, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about what was expected made me avoid logging in because it felt overwhelming. I had other responsibilities that took priority over coursework a lot of the time. The fatigue was real and it showed in how little I engaged.
Q14: Oh for sure. I'd log in, click through some stuff, maybe start a video, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. It was like going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes and couldn't remember what I'd just read. That happened more often than I'd like to admit.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important but I'd put them off because they felt too big or I didn't know where to start. The anxiety about doing them wrong made me avoid them even more. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. It's a bad cycle I couldn't break out of.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd cram everything into the last possible moment, which meant my work wasn't great. The LMS would show me being super active right before due dates and basically absent otherwise. Without deadlines I probably wouldn't have done anything at all honestly.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was usually too late to change much. Sometimes seeing a bad grade made me want to try harder, but that motivation faded quick. Other times negative feedback just made me feel worse and less likely to engage. I didn't really seek out feedback proactively.
Q18: The structure felt kind of rigid and didn't match how I learn. Everything was online and reading-based which isn't my style at all. If there had been more hands-on stuff or practical applications, I might've been more into it. The weekly modules felt repetitive and I lost track of where I was supposed to be.
Q19: The lack of interactive or practical elements made it harder for me. Everything felt very passive, like I was just supposed to absorb information without doing anything with it. The navigation was fine I guess, but nothing about the design made me want to spend time there. More variety would've helped.
Q20: I think some students might be like me, clicking around trying to figure things out but never actually getting to the submission part. Or maybe they're reading and watching stuff but struggling to produce work. High activity doesn't mean understanding. You can spend hours lost in the system without accomplishing anything real.
Q21: That kind of reflects what I was saying about offline studying. Some people are efficient, they grab what they need and work elsewhere. Or they already know the material from somewhere else. I tried to do that but my performance didn't really back it up, so I guess it works better for some people than others.
Q22: The log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you actually understood anything. It misses all the offline work, the thinking, the conversations with classmates. It also can't tell if you were actually engaged or just had the page open while doing something else. Numbers don't capture the full picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort because you want to, not just because you have to. It's about being mentally present and trying to connect with the material. For me, I was rarely there. I was just trying to survive the course, not really engage with it.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how disconnected I felt or how much I was struggling with motivation. It can't capture the anxiety I felt every time I thought about the course. My personal circumstances, the financial stress, feeling like I didn't belong there, none of that shows up in login counts. The human side of being a student is invisible to the system.
